---
title: "Course Overview"
---

## Audience Split

The day is designed for a mixed audience:

- all attendees in the morning
- advanced users in the afternoon

## Session Map

| Step | Focus | Intended outcome |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Sign in and orient | Everyone can access the interface and understand the main surfaces |
| 2 | First agent | Everyone creates a working agent with instructions only |
| 3 | Files as knowledge | Everyone connects a document or spreadsheet |
| 4 | Web knowledge | Everyone configures a website-backed knowledge source |
| 5 | Available MCP servers | Everyone understands what MCP adds to agents |
| 6 | Publishing channels | Everyone sees how agents can be shared |
| 7 | Dev environment | Advanced users install VS Code, `uv`, and Copilot |
| 8 | Build an MCP server | Advanced users expose two tools from Python |
| 9 | Deploy on Azure | Advanced users understand the deployment shape |
| 10 | Connect to Copilot Studio | Advanced users attach the server as a remote capability |
| 11 | Brainstorming | Teams sketch their own internal use cases |

## Variation Strategy

We want variety in selected moments rather than forcing every attendee through the exact same assets.

:::: {.choice-grid}
::: {.choice-card}
### File grounding

Attendees can choose between a Word brief, a spreadsheet about solar capacity, or a time-series pricing file.
:::

::: {.choice-card}
### Website grounding

Attendees can test a utility-style public website, a policy-style page, or a facts-and-FAQ page.
:::

::: {.choice-card}
### MCP examples

The afternoon can use one canonical walkthrough while still offering alternative template servers afterwards.
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
## Public repo rule

The site and sample content should stay generic and reusable. The real client context informs the examples, but it should not appear by name in the repo.
:::
